chunk_summary: Exclusive breastfeeding involves feeding infants only breast milk, with no other liquids or solids except for medicines or oral rehydration solutions when necessary. Adequacy is assessed clinically through feeding patterns, output, and weight trajectory, with supplementation used only when medically required.
learning_objectives: Define exclusive breastfeeding and its clinical parameters for adequacy assessment.
Identify early red flags indicating inadequate breastfeeding or intake.
Understand the hierarchy of supplementation options and strategies to maintain milk supply.
key_facts: Exclusive breastfeeding means the infant receives only breast milk, with no other liquids or solids except medicines or oral rehydration solutions.
Adequacy is assessed by frequent feeds with audible swallowing, appropriate urine and stool output, and stable weight trajectory after initial physiologic loss.
Early red flags include poor latch, inadequate diaper counts, lethargy, worsening jaundice, or excessive weight loss.
Supplementation should prioritize expressed breast milk, then donor milk, and formula only as a last resort.
Supplementation must be time-bound and paired with strategies to protect and rebuild milk supply to maintain exclusivity.
reference_quote: Exclusive breastfeeding means the infant receives only breast milk (including expressed milk), with no other liquids or solids except medicines and oral rehydration solution when indicated. (WHO fact sheet)
document_key_topics: Early warning signs of inadequate breastfeeding
